text: In today's presentation I hope to share some principles of safe adhesiolosis in laparoscopic colorectal surgery. This elderly patient has a mid to distal transverse colon cancer and had previous abdominal surgery. We planned to do an extended right hemicolectomy, but encountered dense abdominal adhesions. Initial entry only allowed one further port, however, clearance of some adhesion allowed us to put another port in. We always start our dissection as close to the abdominal wall as possible. Loose areolar tissue is always a good guide. it's important to break through the loose areolar tissue and create windows. This helps with further safe port placement. We are always wary of loops of small bowel creeping in and hiding behind the omental adhesions. If faced by such a situation, we dissect on both sides of the loop of the small bowel before taking the main loop off the abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, maintaining proximity to the abdominal wall. The dissection navigates through loose areolar tissue, with caution to avoid trauma. Due to thickened midline adhesions, the surgeon primarily uses one instrument to dissect the dense omental adhesions.

Once again, we are always looking for hidden small loops of bowel which can creep up on you any time. In doing adhesiolosis for laparoscopic colorectal surgery, try to preserve the planes that would be used later on for the colonic mobilization. Any altercation with these planes can make a difference to the quality of surgery later on.

The surgeon releases the greater omentum from the pelvic cavity and right colon, restoring the distorted colonic anatomy. The transverse colon is pushed down over the liver to expose the transverse myelocolon. Adhesions are carefully dissected from the right colon and terminal ileum, revealing the small bowel loops hitched to the abdominal wall.
In situations like these, planes can easily be transgressed. It is therefore important to restore the anatomy as much as possible for facilitation of further cancer surgery.

The surgeon begins the operation by identifying and dividing the ileocolic pedicle, developing a plane between the retroperitoneal structures and the right mesocolon. Sharp and blunt dissection techniques are used to protect the duodenum and the head of the pancreas while dissecting the right colonic vessels. The transverse mesocolon is flipped on top of the liver to expose and dissect the middle colic artery, which is shown dividing into right and left branches before being taken close to its origin.

The right colon is fully mobilized, with the transverse colon appearing mobile following the division of the mid-colic artery. The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum from the left side, separating the stomach from the transverse colon. The procedure then shifts to the right side of the patient, continuing to open the gastrocolic omentum and progressing toward the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs a lateral to medial mobilization of the left colon to properly take down the splenic flexure. The splenic flexure is challenging due to multiple layers of adhesion between the spleen and the colon.

The surgeon completes mobilization of the splenic flexure and divides the bowel at the descending colon. The terminal ileum is also divided, and an extracorporeal anastomosis is performed to join the ends together.
